t: Bipolar Type II depressive episodes often with atypical features — hypersomnia, hyperphagia, leaden paralysis, rejection sensitivity.
The patient experiences depressive episodes for more than 70% of their lifetime (Judd L.L. et al. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2003 follow-up).
At least one major depressive episode.
Hypomanic and depressive episodes are not better explained by schizoaffective disorder, schizophrenia, or another psychotic disorder.
Symptoms cause significant distress or functional impairment (primarily from depressive episode).
functional impairment in hypomanic episode should not be significant; antidepressant-induced hypomanic episode leads to bipolar Type II diagnosis (especially in persistent or recurrent cases).
Hirschfeld R.M. reviews — ~25–50% of patients presenting with a depressive episode are actually Bipolar Type II, but hypomanic episodes are perceived by the family as "good periods."
Structured inquiry for hypomanic episodes in every depressive patient (SCID-5 mood module, MDQ, HCL-32).
Information from family members — observation of hypomanic episodes is important.
Laboratory: thyroid, liver, kidney, toxicology, pregnancy test.
≥1 manic episode (functional impairment or hospitalization requirement).
No full major depressive or hypomanic episodes.
Emotional lability hours; identity disturbance; chronic suicidal ideation.
SCID-5 mood module — the gold standard.
MDQ (Mood Disorder Questionnaire) and HCL-32 (Hypomania Checklist) — screening.
Bipolar Type II depressive episode (most common presentation):
First-line — quetiapine (FDA approved), lurasidone, or quetiapine + lithium combination.
Lamotrigine — first-line in maintenance and depressive prophylaxis (particularly effective in Type II).
Stop antidepressant; mood stabilizer (lithium, valproate) or atypical antipsychotic.
formerly considered relatively safe in bipolar Type II, but recent guidelines (CANMAT 2018) recommend only with a mood stabilizer (to reduce risk of manic switch and rapid cycling).
Lamotrigine (when the depressive component is dominant) or quetiapine; lithium for suicide reduction effect.
valproate absolute contraindication; lamotrigine and quetiapine relatively safe.
Type II depressive episode first-line — quetiapine; lamotrigine in maintenance.
Type I and II same main approach, but lamotrigine's specific role emphasized in Type II.
Lamotrigine efficacy in maintenance treatment of bipolar disorder type II.

— Bipolar Type II effective in maintenance and prevention of depressive episodes; titration slow (risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome) — starting dose 25 mg, increase over 2 weeks; target 200 mg/day. Calabrese J.R. et al. J Clin Psychiatry 2003.
— FDA approval for bipolar depressive episode; 300–600 mg/day; metabolic side effect monitoring.
Interpersonal and Social Rhythm Therapy (IPSRT) — Frank (Frank E.); and CBT for bipolar disorder.
Mood Disorder Questionnaire (MDQ — Mood Disorder Questionnaire)
Episodic course; relapse significantly reduced with maintenance pharmacotherapy.
Type II is not 'milder' than Type I — high functional impairment and depressive burden.
Quetiapine and lamotrigine in maintenance as main.
Monitoring — depressive symptoms, hypomanic triggers, metabolic indicators.
Myth 1: "Type II bipolar is a 'mild form' requiring little treatment"
Judd L.L. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2003 — Type II patient spends more than 70% of lifetime in depressive state; functional impairment and suicide risk comparable to Type I.
Myth 2: "Hypomanic episode is a 'good period', no treatment needed"
hypomanic episode can involve risky behavior, impaired relationships, later productivity decline, and trigger depressive episode; isolated episodes indicate need for maintenance pharmacotherapy.
Myth 3: "Antidepressant alone can be used in Type II patient"
CANMAT 2018 — antidepressant only with mood stabilizer; risk of manic switch and rapid cycling.
Myth 4: "Bipolar Type II is only a form of borderline personality disorder"
There is significant overlap and comorbidity, but these are distinct disorders; bipolar Type II episodic, BPD continuous pattern.
Lamotrigine is effective in the depressive component and maintenance; ineffective for manic episodes — antipsychotic or valproate is required.
Myth 6: "St John's Wort is safe in bipolar because it is a natural antidepressant"
St. John's Wort can induce mania and decrease the levels of mood stabilizers (CYP induction); extrapyramidal in bipolar disorder.
Myth 7: ECT is not needed in Type II
ECT indicated for refractory depressive episode or high suicidality risk; also effective in Type II patient.
WHO. ICD-11. 6A61 Bipolar type II disorder. 2024.
Yatham L.N. et al. CANMAT and ISBD 2018 guidelines. Bipolar Disord 2018;20(2):97–170.

Judd L.L., Akiskal H.S., Schettler P.J. et al. A prospective investigation of the natural history of the long-term weekly symptomatic status of bipolar II disorder. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2003;60(3):261–269.

Calabrese J.R. et al. A placebo-controlled 18-month trial of lamotrigine and lithium maintenance treatment in recently depressed patients with bipolar I disorder. J Clin Psychiatry 2003;64(9):1013–1024.
Merikangas K.R. et al. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2011;68(3):241–251.
Angst J. et al. The HCL-32: towards a self-assessment tool for hypomanic symptoms. J Affect Disord 2005;88(2):217–233.
